retrieved_docs.title: Bennett Fracture -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: The range of motion exercises may begin 5 to 10 days post screw fixation and after four weeks after pinning (after the pins are removed). [12] Long-term outcomes based on the study of Kjaer-Petersen which reviewed 41 Bennett fractures note that based on the quality of the fracture reduction. Eighty-six percent of patients with an anatomic reduction (less than 1 mm step off) had no residual symptoms, where only 46% of patients with good or poor reduction (greater than 1 mm step off) remained asymptomatic. [6] Definitive treatment algorithms are lacking due to the small number of patients that are encountered with this fracture, lack of long-term imaging follow up, and lack of randomized prospective data.

retrieved_docs.title: Amsler Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Amsler sign, also known as Amsler-Verrey sign, is present in Fuchs heterochromic uveitis. This sign is characterized by hemorrhage in the anterior chamber (hyphema) following anterior chamber paracentesis or entry with the microvitreoretinal blade during anterior segment surgeries, including cataract surgery or minor trauma. This hemorrhage is due to fine, fragile vessels in the anterior chamber angle. The sign derives its name from Dr Marc Amsler and Dr Florian Verrey, who described it in 1946. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Modified Ashworth Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The assessment begins with the joint in either a fully flexed or fully extended position, depending on the muscle group being tested. The examiner should passively move the limb through its full range of motion at a controlled speed, ideally completing the movement within approximately 1 second. The movement should be performed smoothly and without excessive force.

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Post-rotational nystagmus: reflexive horizontal nystagmus that occurs with a rapid brake to a unidirectional head rotation, with a contralateral fast phase and a subjective rocking boat sensation

retrieved_docs.title: Halo Brace -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Examples of definitive treatment are occipital condyle fractures, occipitocervical dislocation, C1 fractures (most common), and C2 fractures, with an anticipated average healing time of 3 to 4 months. [3] Although this technique demonstrates an approximate success rate of 85%, the effectiveness of the halo brace hinges on appropriate indication, application, and management. [4] Risks are associated with the use of halo braces as a definitive treatment, especially among older patients, necessitating caution in specific populations. [5] In addition, this device can be used in the pediatric population for cervical spine trauma (definitive or conjunction with surgical management), severe scoliosis, and arthrodesis, although this entails adjustments like utilizing more pins and applying reduced insertion torque force to accommodate differences in skull thickness.

retrieved_docs.title: Modified Ashworth Scale -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: 0: No increase in muscle tone 1: Slight increase in muscle tone, with a catch and release or minimal resistance at the end of the range of motion when an affected part(s) is moved in flexion or extension 1+: Slight increase in muscle tone, manifested as a catch, followed by minimal resistance through the remainder (less than half) of the range of motion 2: A marked increase in muscle tone throughout most of the range of motion, but affected part(s) are still easily moved 3: Considerable increase in muscle tone, passive movement difficult 4: Affected part(s) rigid in flexion or extension

retrieved_docs.title: Jackson Cross Cylinder -- Introduction -- Astigmatism
retrieved_docs.content: To understand the JCC and its principle, it is important to take a brief look at the concept of astigmatism. [6] The word " astigmatism " comes from a combination of the English article " a, " which in this case means "without," and the greek word " stigma ," which means "point." In combining the words, the definition indicates the concept of "without a point."

retrieved_docs.title: Lensmaker’s Equation -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lensmaker equation is a mathematical computation to determine the focal length of a lens in air. The focal length of a lens is defined as the distance from the center of the lens to an optical point to which all light rays are refracted. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Riesman Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The name comes from Dr. Herman Snellen and Dr. David Riesman. Dr. Herman Snellen (1834 - 1908) was a famous ophthalmologist from the Netherlands known for Snellen's chart for estimating the visual acuity. Dr. Ravid Riesman (1867-1940) was born in Germany. He completed his MD from the University of Pennsylvania, USA. He was a professor of clinical medicine and then a professor of history of medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: Trendelenburg Sign -- Definition/Introduction -- Prerequisites of Testing
retrieved_docs.content: The patient can stand on the affected side for more than 30 seconds The patient can understand commands The patient has intact coordination The patient has free abduction movement; no fixed adduction deformity

retrieved_docs.title: Humphrey Visual Field -- Normal and Critical Findings -- Anderson and Patella Criteria (Also Called Hodapp-Parrish-Anderson Criteria)
retrieved_docs.content: Observe the following in the visual field printout on at least 2 fields: Abnormal GHT Three or more contiguous non-edge points in a location typical for glaucoma with a P -value of less than 5%, out of which at least 1 point has a P -value of less than 1% Corrected pattern standard deviation should be abnormal with a P -value of less than 5% [37]

retrieved_docs.title: Convergence Insufficiency -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: A definite diagnosis of CI requires specific physical evaluation criterion including: Exophoria (XP) greater at near than far by at least 4 prism diopters Receded Near Point of Convergence (NPC) of 6 cm or more Insufficient Positive Fusional Vergence (PFV) to meet Sheard's Criterion Symptomatic according to the Convergence Insufficiency Symptom Survey (CISS)

retrieved_docs.title: Westphal Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Westphal’s sign refers to a decreased or absent patellar tendon reflex, also known as the knee-jerk reflex, observed on physical exam. The patellar tendon reflex refers to a kicking-like motion produced by the extension of the knee joint upon the ipsilateral stimulation of the patellar tendon. The reflex is thought to play a role in maintaining balance. The realization of the importance of deep tendon reflexes in the physical exam is often attributed to Wilhelm Heinrich Erb and Carl Friedrich Otto Westphal, who both noted this finding in the late 1800s. [1] [2] Westphal’s sign thus receives its name from Carl Friedrich Otto Westphal. The patellar tendon reflex is a deep tendon reflex, similar to the biceps, brachioradialis, triceps, Achilles, and jaw jerk reflexes. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Physiologic end-point nystagmus: horizontal jerks nystagmus results from testing oculomotor movements too far laterally

retrieved_docs.title: Nystagmus Types -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Per-rotational nystagmus: horizontal jerk nystagmus that occurs with sustained head rotations, with fast phases ipsilateral to the rotation

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Angle of Louis -- Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The eponymous name "angle of Louis” is believed to have originated from either Antoine Louis, a French clinician, or Wilhelm Friedrich von Ludwig, a German physician. However, there is no definitive evidence of either origin, and some even speculate that the name originates from another doctor, Pierre Charles Alexandre Louis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 256
retrieved_docs.content: diverse injury types (intracranial, skeletal, facial), their specific anatomical subcategories, corresponding treatment modalities, and long‑term sequelae are interrelated, while also incorporating psychosocial determinants such as aggression that may influence injury occurrence and outcome. This integrated framework supports comprehensive assessment, classification, and management of patients presenting with complex traumatic or toxic presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2031
retrieved_docs.content: Glaucoma includes the SMQ entity الزَرَق, and the entity الزَرَق is classified as a member of the broader group بضع الزاوية, indicating that the specific concept الزَرَق falls within the larger category بضع الزاوية while being a component of glaucoma.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 784
retrieved_docs.content: Complication is linked to multiple open wounds that have not yet resulted in additional problems, and it is also associated with open wounds of the extremities. This relationship underscores that even when open wounds—particularly numerous ones or those located on the limbs—have not produced further complications, they are still considered relevant to the broader concept of complication in clinical assessment and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 208
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the knowledge network unifies head and neck trauma, intracranial and skull‑base injuries, all skeletal fractures, their associated tissue damage, poisoning mechanisms, external causes (MVT, aircraft crash), procedural treatments, and the spectrum of late‑effect sequelae under the central concepts of fracture and late effects of unspecified injury, providing a comprehensive framework for injury classification, management, and long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2346

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses a range of specific adverse‑event concepts relevant to children, and it includes the concept تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse). By incorporating this particular adverse event, the terminology provides clinicians and researchers with a standardized term for identifying, reporting, and managing umbilical cord prolapse in pediatric patients, thereby supporting consistent communication and improved patient safety.

This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.content: Other and ill-defined heart disease is synonymous with تمدد الأوعية الدموية في القلب, a term that describes dilation of the blood vessels within the heart. This equivalence indicates that the ambiguous classification of certain cardiac conditions corresponds to a pathological enlargement of the coronary or myocardial vasculature, which can affect cardiac function and warrants appropriate diagnostic evaluation and management.

Wrist injury is classified both as fractured wrist bone and as a closed distal forearm fracture, reinforcing the hierarchical relationship between wrist, hand, and general limb fracture classifications.
